name: Lapurdian Basque (Lapurtera)

ancestors.notes: Standard Basque base; Lapurdian is the dialect closest to classical written Basque

notes: Lapurdian Basque (Lapurtera / Labourdian). Spoken in Labourd/Lapurdi, French Basque Country (around Bayonne, Biarritz, Saint-Jean-de-Luz). Classical literary dialect: Axular's Gero (1643) is the canonical Basque prose text, written in Lapurdian. KEY FEATURES: (1) FULL ASPIRATE /h/: Unlike Bizkaian, /h/ is preserved and phonemically active in all positions. (2) NO ASPIRATED STOPS: Unlike Souletin, stops p/t/k are NOT aspirated. (3) FULL SIBILANT CONTRAST: s̺/s̻/ʃ and ts̺/ts̻/tʃ are maintained (six-way opposition as in Batua). (4) PALATAL STOPS: [c, ɟ] maintained. (5) FRENCH INFLUENCE: younger speakers may show uvular [ʁ] for /r/ and /ɾ/ under French influence — otherwise alveolar [r]/[ɾ]. (6) CONSERVATIVE MORPHOLOGY: Lapurdian retains some features lost in Batua standardisation; closest to classical Basque literary norm. (7) DECLENSION: full Basque case system maintained; some dialectal case endings differ from Batua.

sources.author: Hualde, J.I. & Ortiz de Urbina, J. (eds.)
sources.title: A Grammar of Basque

sources.author: Oyharçabal, B.
sources.title: The Dialects of the Basque Language
sources.publisher: In: A Grammar of Basque, Mouton de Gruyter
